skill.name: biopython-phylo
skill.description: Use Bio.Phylo to read/write phylogenetic trees and perform visualization and statistics; use when tree parsing/conversion, pruning/rerooting, distance calculation, or plotting is required.
skill.summary: Use Bio.Phylo to read/write phylogenetic trees, perform visualization and statistics, and manipulate tree topology.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 138 static findings are false positives. The 45 high-severity 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are misidentified Bio.Phylo method and property names (e.g., tree.root, tree.depths) in documentation code examples; no cryptographic operations exist. The 88 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are standard Markdown fenced code blocks (triple backticks) in documentation, not shell execution. The 'system reconnaissance' findings reference a JSON audit report, not actual system discovery. The 'hardcoded URL' is a source attribution link. The 'filesystem' findings are legitimate file I/O operations for reading and writing phylogenetic tree data files. Prior evaluation confirms: all 20 assertions pass, status is Production Ready, grade 86/100.

content.user_title: Analyze and visualize phylogenetic trees with Bio.Phylo
content.value_statement: Researchers need to parse, manipulate, and visualize phylogenetic trees for evolutionary analysis. This skill provides expert guidance on using Bio.Phylo for tree reading, writing, format conversion, pruning, rerooting, distance computation, and publication-ready plotting.
actual_capabilities: Parse and write phylogenetic trees in Newick, NEXUS, phyloXML, NeXML, and CDAO formats using Bio.Phylo
Traverse trees and find specific clades using built-in traversal methods
Prune taxa, reroot trees, and modify tree topology with Phylo manipulation functions
Compute pairwise distances, distance matrices, and basic tree statistics
Generate ASCII tree renderings for terminal output and Matplotlib plots for publications
Build trees from distance matrices and multiple sequence alignments
limitations: Cannot perform sequence alignment or molecular evolution model selection
Tree construction is limited to distance-based methods such as UPGMA and neighbor-joining
Does not support Bayesian or maximum likelihood phylogenetic inference
Visualization options are basic compared to dedicated phylogenetics software

use_cases.title: Convert phylogenetic trees between formats
use_cases.description: Transform tree files between Newick, NEXUS, phyloXML, and other common formats for interoperability between different bioinformatics tools.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics researcher handling phylogenetic data from multiple sources

use_cases.title: Compute phylogenetic diversity metrics
use_cases.description: Calculate pairwise distances, distance matrices, branch length summaries, and phylogenetic diversity from tree data for evolutionary analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Evolutionary biologist studying species relationships and biodiversity

use_cases.title: Generate publication-ready tree figures
use_cases.description: Create high-resolution tree visualizations with Matplotlib for research papers, presentations, and scientific reports.
use_cases.target_user: Data analyst or researcher preparing scientific publications

prompt_templates.title: Read and visualize a phylogenetic tree
prompt_templates.prompt: Read a phylogenetic tree file in Newick format using Bio.Phylo and display it as ASCII output.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time users who need to load and visualize a tree quickly

prompt_templates.title: Convert tree file format
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert a phylogenetic tree from Newick format to phyloXML format using Bio.Phylo and verify the output.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate users needing to transform tree files for different tools

prompt_templates.title: Prune and reroot a tree
prompt_templates.prompt: Load a phylogenetic tree, prune specific taxa by name, reroot the tree using an outgroup species, and save the modified tree.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researchers refining a tree by removing taxa and setting a new root

prompt_templates.title: Build a tree from sequence alignment
prompt_templates.prompt: Read a multiple sequence alignment file, calculate a distance matrix, construct a neighbor-joining tree, and save it with visual output.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced users constructing phylogenetic trees from sequence data

output_examples.input: Read a Newick tree file and display it as ASCII.
output_examples.output: An ASCII tree diagram showing the phylogenetic relationships between taxa with branch lengths, rendered as text characters in the terminal.

output_examples.input: Calculate pairwise distances between all taxa in a tree.
output_examples.output: A distance matrix table showing evolutionary distances between each pair of taxa, with row and column headers matching taxon names.

output_examples.input: Generate a publication-quality tree figure with Matplotlib.
output_examples.output: A high-resolution PNG image of the phylogenetic tree with labeled taxa, branch lengths, and a clear title, suitable for inclusion in research papers.

best_practices: Always use explicit file encoding when reading and writing tree files to ensure consistent character handling across platforms.
Create copies of trees using the .copy() method before making modifications to preserve the original tree structure.
Choose the appropriate file format based on annotation needs: Newick for simple topologies, phyloXML for rich metadata and annotations.
anti_patterns: Modifying the original tree directly without creating a copy first, which can lead to data loss if the modification produces unwanted results.
Using inconsistent taxon names across input files, causing lookup failures when finding clades or computing distances.
Ignoring missing branch length values when computing statistics, which results in incorrect calculations and misleading summary metrics.

faq.question: What file formats does Bio.Phylo support?
faq.answer: Bio.Phylo supports Newick, NEXUS, phyloXML, NeXML, and CDAO formats for reading and writing phylogenetic trees.

faq.question: How do I convert between tree formats?
faq.answer: Use Phylo.convert() to directly convert between formats, or use Phylo.read() followed by Phylo.write() for more control over the conversion.

faq.question: How do I prune specific taxa from a tree?
faq.answer: Use tree.prune(target) with the taxon name to remove a specific leaf. Iterate over all terminals to prune multiple taxa as needed.

faq.question: How do I root a phylogenetic tree?
faq.answer: Use tree.root_at_midpoint() for midpoint rooting, or tree.root_with_outgroup() to root using a specific outgroup species.

faq.question: How do I compute pairwise distances between taxa?
faq.answer: Use tree.distance(taxon1, taxon2) to compute the distance between two taxa. Iterate over all terminal pairs to build a full distance matrix.

faq.question: How do I create publication-quality tree figures?
faq.answer: Use Phylo.draw() with Matplotlib to create high-resolution figures. Set the Agg backend for headless environments and use savefig() to export PNG files.
